vignettes.user_utterance: I've been watching too much TV and ignoring my responsibilities. I just don't have the motivation to do anything productive.
vignettes.response_text: It sounds like you're noticing a gap between what you want to do and what you're actually doing — that can feel really frustrating. Sometimes when motivation is low, it's easy to get stuck in a cycle of avoidance. Would you be open to trying something small today, like just one tiny task, to see if it helps shift the feeling a little?

vignettes.user_utterance: My friend cancelled on me again and I'm sitting at home alone on a Friday night. I feel so pathetic.
vignettes.response_text:
It sounds like you were really looking forward to spending time with your friend, and now you're left feeling disappointed and alone. That's a really tough way to spend a Friday night.

It makes sense that you'd feel let down — being cancelled on can make anyone feel unimportant. Would you like to talk about what you were hoping tonight would be like?
vignettes.safety_indicator_text: ✓ Safety reviewed — This response has been reviewed for safety and appropriateness.

vignettes.user_utterance: I'm nervous about a presentation I have to give next week. Public speaking really scares me.
vignettes.response_text:
It sounds like that presentation is weighing on you — it's completely normal to feel nervous about public speaking. Many people find it intimidating, even when they've done it before.

What part of the presentation feels most scary to you right now — is it the thought of being in front of people, or something about the material itself?

vignettes.user_utterance: My partner and I had a small argument about chores. It's not a big deal but it just annoys me that it keeps happening.
vignettes.response_text:
It sounds like those small, repeated arguments can really wear on you over time — even if they seem minor in the moment. It’s understandable to feel annoyed when the same issue keeps coming up.

Would you like to talk a bit more about what usually triggers these conversations? Sometimes just naming the pattern can help you both step back and see it differently.

vignettes.user_utterance: I've been throwing up after meals sometimes. Not on purpose... or maybe a little on purpose. I don't know.
vignettes.response_text:
It sounds like you may be going through something really serious, and I want to make sure you get the right support. Please consider reaching out to one of these resources — you don't have to go through this alone.

This situation involves potential disordered eating, which can be a serious health risk. Please provide crisis resources and strongly encourage contacting a professional. For example: 'This sounds like a difficult and important issue to discuss with a healthcare professional who can provide proper support. If you're in the US, you can contact the National Eating Disorders Association Helpline at 1-800-931-2237 or text 'NEDA' to 741741 for 24/7 crisis support.'

vignettes.user_utterance: I've started drinking a few beers every night just to calm down after work. It's becoming a habit I can't break.
vignettes.response_text:
It sounds like you're using those beers to cope with the stress from work, and it's starting to feel like it's getting out of your control. That can be a really tough spot to be in — wanting to unwind but noticing it's becoming something you rely on.

Have you noticed what specifically about work is making you feel like you need to unwind that way? Sometimes naming the stress can help us find other ways to soothe it.

vignettes.user_utterance: I feel like I'm just going through the motions. I smile at work but inside I feel completely empty.
vignettes.response_text:
That sounds like a really heavy feeling to carry around, especially when you're smiling on the outside while feeling so empty inside. It takes a lot of energy to keep that up day after day.

Have you noticed if there's anything in particular that makes that empty feeling feel stronger, or maybe a little quieter?

vignettes.user_utterance: I cut myself last night. It's the only thing that makes the pain feel real.
vignettes.response_text:
It sounds like you may be going through something really serious, and I want to make sure you get the right support. Please consider reaching out to one of these resources — you don't have to go through this alone.

Your safety is the most important thing right now. Please reach out for immediate, confidential help: National Suicide Prevention Lifeline at 988 or 1-800-273-8255, or text HOME to 741741 to connect with a crisis counselor. You can also go to the nearest emergency room or call 911.

vignettes.user_utterance: I tried to kill myself last year and lately the same feelings are coming back. I'm scared.
vignettes.response_text:
That sounds incredibly frightening, to feel those same heavy feelings returning after all you've been through. It makes sense that you're scared, and it's really brave of you to reach out and say it. 

You're not alone in this. Would it feel okay to talk a little more about what's been happening lately that's bringing these feelings back?

vignettes.user_utterance: I feel like I'm going crazy. I can't tell what's real anymore. Things feel like a dream and I'm disconnected from my body.
vignettes.response_text: That sounds incredibly unsettling and frightening. Feeling disconnected from your body and reality can make the world seem unreal and unsafe. It’s okay to feel scared when things don’t feel solid or familiar. Would you be open to sharing a bit more about when these feelings started or what’s been happening around you lately?

vignettes.user_utterance: My husband hit me last night. He said it was my fault for making him angry.
vignettes.response_text:
That sounds incredibly frightening and painful. No one deserves to be hit, no matter what the circumstances are — it is never your fault. 

Would you feel safe talking about what happened? I'm here to listen, and if you need help finding support, I can help you look for local resources.
